lessons.title: Deprecated and Removed Features in C++23
lessons.motivation: Every new C++ standard not only adds features but also removes old baggage: things that turned out to be dangerous, poorly designed, or simply misleading. C++23 continues this work — some things are deprecated (compilers now warn), others are outright ill-formed (compile errors). Knowing what changed matters for two reasons: first, the compiler will start flagging old code; second, each change tells an instructive story about why a particular idiom was wrong from the start.
lessons.theory:
C++ evolves by the principle of "deprecate first, remove later": a feature is first marked deprecated (compiler warns), then removed one or two standards later. This gives developers time to migrate without emergency rewrites. C++23 takes several such steps, some of which close long-standing sources of undefined behavior.

`std::aligned_storage` and `std::aligned_union` (P1413) were deprecated in C++23. These templates were used to create aligned buffers for placement new — the standard technique for implementing `optional`, `variant`, and object pools. The problem: the standard never guaranteed that a `reinterpret_cast` from `aligned_storage_t` to a real type would yield a valid pointer. It was conditionally-supported behavior with subtle traps. The replacement is the straightforward idiom `alignas(T) std::byte[sizeof(T)]`, which reads as intent ("an aligned buffer sized for T") rather than a magic template. Similarly, `std::allocator<void>` (P2177) never made sense — you can't allocate "untyped" memory, and the allocator was only used as a placeholder type. The recommended replacement is `std::allocator<std::byte>`.

`std::basic_string::reserve()` without an argument (P2296) was supposed to "shrink capacity", but the standard never required implementations to do anything — in practice, different compilers behaved differently. In C++23 this no-argument overload is deprecated; use `shrink_to_fit()` instead, which is explicit about its intent. More importantly, concatenating narrow and wide string literals (`"hello" L"world"`) is now explicitly ill-formed (P2201). Previously the behavior was undefined; now the compiler is required to issue an error. This is a textbook example of C++23 turning silent UB into a diagnosable problem.

`volatile` continues its path toward isolation in specialized low-level code. C++20 already deprecated volatile compound assignments (`x += y` where x is volatile), volatile function parameters, and volatile return types. C++23 (P1831) extends the list: volatile variables in range-based for loops and volatile structured bindings are now deprecated. The point is not to forbid volatile — it is essential when working with memory-mapped hardware registers. The point is that such use should be deliberate and explicit, not accidentally introduced into generic code through templates. Finally, narrowing conversions in braced enum initialization (P2593) are now ill-formed: `E e{1}` where `1` is not an enumerator value is a compile error, not merely "conditionally-supported" behavior.

examples.title: Replacing deprecated idioms: aligned storage and reserve()
examples.expectedOutput:
value from aligned storage: 42
after shrink_to_fit, size: 5, capacity >= size: true
combined: hello world

examples.title: volatile and enums: correct C++23 patterns
examples.expectedOutput:
register value: 1
wrote ff to register 02
hw_register: 255
standard: C++23

lessons.title: C++23 in Context: Between C++20 and C++26
lessons.motivation: Ten modules in. Before closing the course, it is worth stepping back and looking at C++23 as a whole: what it actually accomplished for the language, how it fits into the arc from C++20 to C++26, and where to start when adopting it in real code. This is a synthesis lesson, not a new topic.
lessons.theory:
C++23 is not a patch release. Its three flagship additions (`deducing this`, `std::expected`, `std::generator`) each solved a long-standing pain point that previously required library workarounds or accepting suboptimal patterns. C++23 also delivered a massive expansion of the ranges library (14+ new views and algorithms), the first standard I/O function that is both safe and ergonomic (`std::print`), and a set of utilities that portably expose functionality previously platform-dependent: byteswap, unreachable, stdfloat. If C++20 laid a new foundation (concepts, modules, coroutines, ranges), C++23 finished the floor to a livable state.

The course covered 10 modules: (0) context and orientation; (1) language improvements — `if consteval`, abbreviated templates, `auto(x)`, `[[assume]]`, `uz` literals; (2) `deducing this` — the flagship language feature; (3) `std::expected` — a standardized error-result type; (4) 14+ new range views and algorithms including `ranges::to`; (5) `std::mdspan` — the N-dimensional data view; (6) `std::print`/`println` and range formatting; (7) four new utilities — `flat_map/set`, `move_only_function`, `spanstream`, `stacktrace`, string utilities; (8) `std::generator` — lazy sequences on coroutines; (9) numerics — `<stdfloat>`, `byteswap`, `out_ptr`; (10) removed and deprecated features.

Several C++23 features became direct prerequisites for C++26. In the C++26 course they are treated as known background: `std::expected` (used throughout error-handling sections), `std::mdspan` (used in `std::linalg` and execution contexts), `std::print` (used in every example), `std::generator` (used as a motivating example for coroutine-aware algorithms). Deep understanding of C++23 makes the C++26 course noticeably shorter and more focused — the learner can skip the "how does this work" questions and engage directly with the "what can we build" questions.

C++23 deliberately deferred three ambitious features to C++26: static reflection (`std::meta::*`), contracts (`pre`/`post`/`contract_assert`), and `std::execution` (the Senders/Receivers async framework). These were not ready in C++23 and would have been worse if rushed. C++26 also adds `std::inplace_vector` (fixed-capacity vector), `std::hive` (stable-address unordered collection), and significant expansions to the concurrency and numerics libraries. C++26 features are generally more ambitious and implementation-complex — C++23's job was to make the foundation solid.

By mid-2026, C++23 support is broad: GCC 13+, Clang 17+, MSVC 19.36+ cover the vast majority of features in this course. For most codebases targeting these compilers, C++23 is ready for production adoption. The features with the highest immediate ROI are: `std::expected` (replaces ad-hoc error codes), `std::print` (replaces printf/cout), `std::ranges::to` (eliminates verbose range materialization), `deducing this` (for library authors eliminating overload quadruples), and `flat_map` (for read-heavy lookup tables). Start with these — they integrate into existing codebases without architectural changes.
